paragraphs.body: I have always been fascinated by the minds of serial killers, and how they can commit such a heinous crimes and get away with it for so long. Well actually, there was on specific serial killer that has really caught my attention. Theodore Bundy, commonly referred as “Ted Bundy,” was a famous serial in the 1970’s that admitted to killing thirty women, but experts believe that it was over one hundred. Ted’s childhood was not such a “normal” childhood. His mother was secretly ashamed of her son and wanted to put him up for adoption. His mother’s parents did not want Ted to be put in the system, so they adopted him as their own. They told Ted that his mother was his sister, which had a great effect on him. Growing up Ted was part of the working-class family. He did well in school but not with his peers. At a young age he started showing interest in knives and killings, but no one saw that as odd at the time. Ted soon graduated from the University of Washington in 1972, where he got a degree in psychology. He then was accepted and attended law school in Utah. This package made him seem quite the catch to female colleagues. By using his intelligence and good looks, Ted was able to lore women in and then brutally murder them. It was not until 1976 that he finally got caught and found guilty of his murders.

annotations.comment: This information reads like research/summary, but I don't see who or what you're summarizing. Don't forget the summary templates!

paragraphs.body: When I first watched a documentary about Ted Bundy, I was sitting right beside my mom with both of us in utter shock. His story was not like most serial killers. Instead of hiring a defense attorney to defend his case, Ted decided he was going to represent himself. His trials were broadcasted all over the United States where it caught many people attention. Many people thought Ted was innocent because he looked like a “normal” human being in society. But according to Parrington, “Ted Bundy, who was a “charming, handsome, successful individual [yet also] a sadist, necrophile, rapist, and murderer with zero remorse who took pride in his ability to successfully kill and evade capture.” Parrington’s point is that someone may look like a “normal” human being based on the qualities they showcase, but deep inside there is no such thing as normal.

To me, what people now in days considered as “normal” is an everyday usage of social media. Social media has greatly impacted the world today ever since COVID-19 happened. During that time, I would spend hours scrolling through Tik Tok or Instagram looking at different influencers and how they lived their lives. These social media platforms had consumed me into their façade to the point where I felt that I would never escaped. Social media not only influenced the world around me, but it also impacted on my own mental and physical health. Since I was locked inside my house with nothing to do, I turned to social media to help get a bit of a normalcy back into my life. I spent hours watching girls my age film videos of what they eat in a day, their everyday workout, and how happy they were with the way that they looked. I soon started to compare myself to these girls and started changing myself in order to portray their image. At first it was not a drastic change, I started eating “better” and started joining my mom in on her workouts. Then as time went on and I continued this routine, I started to become very insecure and unhappy by the way I looked. I kept on comparing myself to the influencers online, and how I was following everything that they were doing, but not getting the same results they were. I quickly started to control my consumption of food by only allowing myself to eat one protein bar a day while also spending long hours exercising. In my mind, this was the only way I could be able to obtain the way these influencers looked. This logic soon turned into something that I never saw coming. I was diagnosis with anorexia nervosa six months after I fell into this façade. This disease overtook my life. But I could not turn away from the toll social media had on me. I continue to spend hours on social media, still comparing the way I looked at the time to the girls online. I never felt “skinny” enough compared to these influencers. I knew deep down I had a problem, but I could not escape this feeling of trying to look a certain way. I stayed with the same routine as before, but as time went on, I started eating the same meals every day and had to work out a certain number of hours. This started to cause harm to my physical health. I was tired, cold, and weak all the time, and as the disease further progressed, it started affecting my heart. I did not realize the harm that social media had on me until I was faced with the news that if I continued down this path, I would lose my own life.

annotations.text: These social media platforms had consumed me into their façade

paragraphs.body: When you think about it, the influencers are not actually posting raw and unedited pictures or videos of themselves. Instead, they are posting edited photos and videos of what they want you to see of them, while hiding the reality of their true self. Kind of like Ted Bundy for instance. When you take a moment to considered, Ted Bundy and social media are the same person. They hide under this mask that makes people think of them as appealing to the eye while not noticing their actual motives. They lore young girls in, until they finally become their victim. Social media display multiple qualities of a serial killer, and it makes you consider that is social media a murder? In Parrington’s view, “Serial killers characteristically lack empathy for others, coupled with an apparent absence of guilt about their actions.” In other words, Parrington believes these killers do not care about the harm they inflict upon their victims and the impact they will have on victims lives even if they survive. The social media platform has not shown any sort of sympathy to any of their victims. They do not care that they lore these young girls into their façade that ultimately harm them in the end.

annotations.text: Social media display multiple qualities of a serial killer, and it makes you consider that is social media a murder?

paragraphs.body: It took years of hard work and self-control to become free from the affect social media had on me. I no longer let these influencers and social media have control over my life because I can simply unfollow or delete them anytime, I want to. I now have the strength to continue on with my life, not worrying about how the media precedes girls my age and how they “should” look like. I know now that this is just a façade and not actually reality. After becoming free from social media, I was able to see who I was as a person. I am kind, funny, intelligent, strong, hardworking, caring, but most importantly a survivor.

annotations.text: am kind, funny, intelligent, strong, hardworking, caring, but most importantly a survivor.
